title: Brooklyn Museum

infobox.value: Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences, Brooklyn Museum of Art

**[Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")**: ["2" train](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)")​​["3" train](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)") at [Eastern Parkway–Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway%E2%80%93Brooklyn_Museum_station "Eastern Parkway–Brooklyn Museum station") **[Bus](/wiki/New_York_City_Bus "New York City Bus")**: [B45](/wiki/B45_(New_York_City_bus) "B45 (New York City bus)"), [B48](/wiki/B48_(New_York_City_bus) "B48 (New York City bus)")
[McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White"); French, Daniel Chester

["2" train](/wiki/2_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "2 (New York City Subway service)")​​["3" train](/wiki/3_(New_York_City_Subway_service) "3 (New York City Subway service)") at [Eastern Parkway–Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway%E2%80%93Brooklyn_Museum_station "Eastern Parkway–Brooklyn Museum station") **[Bus](/wiki/New_York_City_Bus "New York City Bus")**: [B45](/wiki/B45_(New_York_City_bus) "B45 (New York City bus)"), [B48](/wiki/B48_(New_York_City_bus) "B48 (New York City bus)") |

is an [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in the [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). At 560,000 square feet (52,000 m2), the museum is New York City's second largest and contains an art collection with around 500,000 objects.[[2]](#cite_note-nyt-2021-11-222-2) Located near the [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn"), [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn"), and [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope "Park Slope") neighborhoods of Brooklyn, the museum's [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") building was designed by [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White").

infobox.key: Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences
infobox.value: in 1843. The museum was conceived as an institution focused on a broad public.[[3]](#cite_note-Lawrence_1987-3) The Brooklyn Museum's current building dates to 1897 and has been expanded several times since then. The museum initially struggled to maintain its building and collection, but it was revitalized in the late 20th century following major renovations.

The **Brooklyn Museum** is an [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in the [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") [borough](/wiki/Borough_(New_York_City) "Borough (New York City)") of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). At 560,000 square feet (52,000 m2), the museum is New York City's second largest and contains an art collection with around 500,000 objects.[[2]](#cite_note-nyt-2021-11-222-2) Located near the [Prospect Heights](/wiki/Prospect_Heights,_Brooklyn "Prospect Heights, Brooklyn"), [Crown Heights](/wiki/Crown_Heights,_Brooklyn "Crown Heights, Brooklyn"), [Flatbush](/wiki/Flatbush,_Brooklyn "Flatbush, Brooklyn"), and [Park Slope](/wiki/Park_Slope "Park Slope") neighborhoods of Brooklyn, the museum's [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") building was designed by [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White").

The Brooklyn Museum was founded in 1823 as the [Brooklyn Apprentices' Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Apprentices%27_Library "Brooklyn Apprentices' Library") and merged with the **Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences** in 1843. The museum was conceived as an institution focused on a broad public.[[3]](#cite_note-Lawrence_1987-3) The Brooklyn Museum's current building dates to 1897 and has been expanded several times since then. The museum initially struggled to maintain its building and collection, but it was revitalized in the late 20th century following major renovations.

Significant areas of the collection include antiquities, specifically their collection of [Egyptian antiquities](/wiki/Egyptian_antiquities "Egyptian antiquities") spanning over 3,000 years. [European](/wiki/Art_of_Europe "Art of Europe"), [African](/wiki/African_art "African art"), [Oceanic](/wiki/Oceanic_art "Oceanic art"), and [Japanese art](/wiki/Japanese_art "Japanese art") make for notable antiquities collections as well. [American art](/wiki/Visual_art_of_the_United_States "Visual art of the United States") is heavily represented, starting at the [Colonial](/wiki/American_colonial_period "American colonial period") period. Artists represented in the collection include [Mark Rothko](/wiki/Mark_Rothko "Mark Rothko"), [Edward Hopper](/wiki/Edward_Hopper "Edward Hopper"), [Norman Rockwell](/wiki/Norman_Rockwell "Norman Rockwell"), [Judy Chicago](/wiki/Judy_Chicago "Judy Chicago"), [Winslow Homer](/wiki/Winslow_Homer "Winslow Homer"), [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe"), and [Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber_(artist) "Max Weber (artist)").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Brooklyn Museum's origins date to August 1823,[[4]](#cite_note-Aldred_Brooklyn_Museum_1973_p.-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Weeks_1894_p._1526-5) when Brooklyn citizens, including Augustus Graham, founded the [Brooklyn Apprentices' Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Apprentices%27_Library "Brooklyn Apprentices' Library") in [Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights").[[6]](#cite_note-j26458466-6)[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) The library was formally incorporated November 24, 1824,[[5]](#cite_note-Weeks_1894_p._1526-5) and the cornerstone of the library's first building was laid in 1825 on Henry and Cranberry Street.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-1898-07-09-8) The Library moved into the [Brooklyn Lyceum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Lyceum "Brooklyn Lyceum") building on Washington Street in 1841.[[9]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum-9) The two institutions merged into the Brooklyn Institute in 1843; the institute offered exhibitions of painting and sculpture and lectures on diverse subjects.[[8]](#cite_note-nyt-1898-07-09-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum-9) The Washington Street building was destroyed in a fire in 1891.[[10]](#cite_note-p126805239-10)

### Development and opening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Development and opening")]

In February 1889, several prominent Brooklyn citizens announced that they would begin fundraising for a new museum for the Brooklyn Institute.[[11]](#cite_note-n127453146-11)[[12]](#cite_note-n127453106-12) The museum's proponents quickly identified a site just east of [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)"), on the south side of [Eastern Parkway](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway "Eastern Parkway").[[13]](#cite_note-n127453194-13) The next year, under director [Franklin Hooper](/wiki/Franklin_Hooper "Franklin Hooper"), Institute leaders reorganized as the Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences and began planning the Brooklyn Museum.[[14]](#cite_note-n127453344-14) Brooklyn officials hosted an [architectural design competition](/wiki/Architectural_design_competition "Architectural design competition") for the building, eventually awarding the contract to [McKim, Mead & White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead_%26_White "McKim, Mead & White").[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) The competition was characterized in the *Brooklyn Daily Eagle* as "one of the most important in the history of architecture", as the museum was to contain numerous divisions.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) The museum remained a subdivision of the Brooklyn Institute of Arts and Sciences, along with the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music"), the [Brooklyn Botanic Garden](/wiki/Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden "Brooklyn Botanic Garden"), and the [Brooklyn Children's Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Children%27s_Museum "Brooklyn Children's Museum"), until these organizations all became independent in the 1970s.[[9]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum-9)

Brooklyn mayor [Charles A. Schieren](/wiki/Charles_A._Schieren "Charles A. Schieren") agreed in January 1895 to issue $300,000 per year in bonds for the Brooklyn Institute museum's construction.[[15]](#cite_note-p573996298-15) Initially, only a single wing and pavilion on the western portion of the museum's site, measuring 210 by 50 feet (64 by 15 m) across, was to be built.[[16]](#cite_note-p574014116-16) Engineers began surveying the site that May[[17]](#cite_note-n127452720-17)[[18]](#cite_note-n127452758-18) and found that the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") under the site was several hundred feet deep, making it impossible to build the foundations on solid rock.[[19]](#cite_note-p574038500-19) Nonetheless, the engineers had determined that the gravel [fill](/wiki/Cut_and_fill "Cut and fill") under the site was strong enough to support a building.[[17]](#cite_note-n127452720-17) Construction on the Brooklyn Museum of Arts and Sciences' west wing officially began on September 14, 1895.[[20]](#cite_note-nyt-1895-09-15-20)[[21]](#cite_note-p574081465-21) A [groundbreaking](/wiki/Groundbreaking "Groundbreaking") ceremony for the museum was hosted on December 14 of the same year.[[22]](#cite_note-n127452867-22)[[23]](#cite_note-nyt-1895-12-15-23) Two of the museum's three stories had been completed by April 1896.[[24]](#cite_note-p574167414-24)

The Brooklyn Institute museum's building was completed in March 1897 after a sidewalk was built between the museum's entrance and Eastern Parkway.[[25]](#cite_note-p574278771-25) The museum's first exhibit was a collection of almost 600 paintings, which had opened to the public on June 1, 1897, several months before the formal opening of the museum.[[26]](#cite_note-p574319190-26) The Brooklyn Institute's museum formally opened on October 2, 1897, and was one of the last major structures built in the city of Brooklyn before the formation of the [City of Greater New York](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") in 1898.[[27]](#cite_note-p574349508-27)[[28]](#cite_note-nyt-1897-10-03-28)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

#### 1900s and 1910s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1900s and 1910s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Brooklyn_Institute_of_Arts_and_Sciences%2C_Brooklyn%2C_N.Y_%28NYPL_b12647398-70040%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Brooklyn_Institute_of_Arts_and_Sciences%2C_Brooklyn%2C_N.Y_%28NYPL_b12647398-70040%29.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Institute_of_Arts_and_Sciences,_Brooklyn,_N.Y_(NYPL_b12647398-70040).tiff)

Early 20th century postcard

The Brooklyn Institute approved the construction of the central entrance pavilion in May 1899,[[29]](#cite_note-p574611963-29) and Hooper requested $600,000 for this addition the next month.[[30]](#cite_note-n127535654-30)[[31]](#cite_note-n127535725-31) The four-story structure was to measure 140 by 122 feet (43 by 37 m).[[32]](#cite_note-n127545424-32)[[33]](#cite_note-n127545591-33) The central pavilion was to include a 1,250-seat lecture hall in the basement (actually at ground level),[[29]](#cite_note-p574611963-29)[[34]](#cite_note-n127544894-34) as well as a hall of sculpture on the first floor, which would serve as the museum's main lobby.[[29]](#cite_note-p574611963-29)[[32]](#cite_note-n127545424-32) The second story was to contain natural-history exhibits, while the third story was to include paintings.[[32]](#cite_note-n127545424-32) The [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") needed to authorize $300,000 in bonds for the pavilion, but they had not done so by the end of 1899.[[35]](#cite_note-n127535886-35) Work on the central wing started in June 1900.[[33]](#cite_note-n127545591-33)[[36]](#cite_note-n127545754-36) The museum's central section was nearly completed by January 1903,[[37]](#cite_note-p571236596-37) but work proceeded slowly due to labor disputes.[[34]](#cite_note-n127544894-34)

New York City mayor [Seth Low](/wiki/Seth_Low "Seth Low") signed a bill in August 1902, approving $150,000 for the construction of the Brooklyn Institute's eastern wing and pavilion.[[38]](#cite_note-nyt-1902-08-12-38) The eastern wing cost $344,000 to construct,[[39]](#cite_note-p572067208-39) and it officially opened on December 14, 1907.[[40]](#cite_note-p571953389-40)[[41]](#cite_note-n127547506-41) With the opening of the eastern wing, the museum building had reached one-eighth of its total planned size.[[42]](#cite_note-n127553386-42) Although the museum's collections continued to grow, the New York City government was only willing to give the museum as little funding as necessary for essential maintenance.[[43]](#cite_note-p508046466-43) Several of the institute's donors proposed in 1905 to give $25,000 for the upkeep of an "astronomical observatory" at the Brooklyn Museum.[[44]](#cite_note-n128728967-44)[[45]](#cite_note-n128776960-45) City officials endorsed the creation of the observatory in 1907.[[46]](#cite_note-n128777045-46)

The Brooklyn Institute awarded a construction contract for wings F and G, extending south of the central pavilion, to Benedetto & Egan in May 1911.[[47]](#cite_note-n127555770-47) Extending 120 feet (37 m) south and measuring 200 feet (61 m) wide, this addition was to contain a central court with a glass roof.[[47]](#cite_note-n127555770-47)[[48]](#cite_note-p574758676-48) That July, McKim, Mead & White filed plans for wings F and G.[[49]](#cite_note-p574788579-49) The Brooklyn Institute converted the last remaining storage rooms in the eastern wing into galleries in October 1911.[[50]](#cite_note-n127555622-50)[[51]](#cite_note-n127555669-51) The next month, a temporary access road was built from [Flatbush Avenue](/wiki/Flatbush_Avenue "Flatbush Avenue") to the rear of the building.[[52]](#cite_note-n127556256-52) Wills & Martin, one of the firms that had been hired to erect the new wings, declared bankruptcy in November 1913.[[53]](#cite_note-n127556455-53) Work stopped completely in November 1914,[[54]](#cite_note-n127745197-54) and the incomplete structures started to deteriorate.[[55]](#cite_note-n127557342-55) Because of the lack of space in the building, the lobby and auditorium were being used to exhibit artwork. The Brooklyn Institute had been forced to decline some donations of artwork, as the works could not be displayed, while other works of art had to be placed in storage.[[55]](#cite_note-n127557342-55)

#### 1920s to 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1920s to 1940s")]

By 1920, the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")'s [Institute Park station](/wiki/Eastern_Parkway%E2%80%93Brooklyn_Museum_station "Eastern Parkway–Brooklyn Museum station") had opened outside the Brooklyn Museum, greatly improving access to the once-isolated museum from [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and the other boroughs.[[56]](#cite_note-56) In April 1922, governor [Nathan L. Miller](/wiki/Nathan_L._Miller "Nathan L. Miller") signed legislation authorizing the New York City government to issue bonds to fund wings F and G of the Brooklyn Museum.[[57]](#cite_note-n127558242-57) The [New York City Board of Estimate](/wiki/New_York_City_Board_of_Estimate "New York City Board of Estimate") refused to approve the Brooklyn Institute trustees' request for $875,000,[[58]](#cite_note-n127558437-58) and mayor [John Francis Hylan](/wiki/John_Francis_Hylan "John Francis Hylan") also blocked the funding.[[59]](#cite_note-n127745094-59) Hylan changed his mind after visiting the museum, and the Board of Estimate appropriated $1.05 million for the new wings.[[60]](#cite_note-n127746541-60) McKim, Mead & White drew up new plans for wings F and G; by that September, [New York City Department of Parks and Recreation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "New York City Department of Parks and Recreation") (NYC Parks) was about to award contracts for the wings.[[61]](#cite_note-n127746255-61)[[62]](#cite_note-n127746120-62) A picture gallery opened at the museum in November 1925.[[63]](#cite_note-nyt-1925-11-15-63)[[64]](#cite_note-n128526319-64) The next month, museum officials dedicated the Ethnological Gallery, which was nicknamed "Rainbow House";[[65]](#cite_note-p1677044361-65)[[66]](#cite_note-n128526988-66) the gallery was designed by curator [Stewart Culin](/wiki/Stewart_Culin "Stewart Culin").[[67]](#cite_note-nyt-1926-11-28-67) A Japanese art gallery opened at the museum in April 1927,[[68]](#cite_note-nyt-1927-04-24-68) and the museum's Swiss Gothic, German, and Venetian galleries opened that May.[[69]](#cite_note-n128622328-69)[[70]](#cite_note-n128527852-70)

Construction of the Brooklyn Museum stalled in 1928 after years of attempts to complete it. At the time, only 28 of the 80 proposed statues atop the building's facade had been installed, and the main north–south corridor was not complete.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) Nineteen American [period rooms](/wiki/Period_room "Period room") opened at the museum at the end of 1929.[[71]](#cite_note-n128528394-71) In May 1934, NYC Parks approved plans for the removal of the main entrance steps,[[72]](#cite_note-n127640281-72) which were replaced by ground-level doors.[[72]](#cite_note-n127640281-72)[[73]](#cite_note-p1223283956-73) The project also included the construction of two galleries next to the lobby,[[74]](#cite_note-p1222340168-74) as well as new landscaping and parking lots.[[73]](#cite_note-p1223283956-73) This work was carried out by [Public Works Administration](/wiki/Public_Works_Administration "Public Works Administration") laborers.[[75]](#cite_note-75) A gallery dedicated to living artists' work opened in February 1935,[[76]](#cite_note-n128528513-76) and a Persian art gallery opened two months later.[[77]](#cite_note-nyt-1935-04-08-77)[[78]](#cite_note-n128530127-78) The remodeled entrance was officially dedicated on October 5, 1935.[[74]](#cite_note-p1222340168-74)[[79]](#cite_note-n128529587-79) That December, the museum's medieval art gallery opened.[[80]](#cite_note-n128529407-80)[[81]](#cite_note-nyt-1935-12-07-81) A gallery for industrial art was also proposed behind the western wing but was not built.[[82]](#cite_note-p1653382463-82) The museum's remodeling was completed in October 1937. Several collections, including Egyptian and Assyrian art, Renaissance art, and textiles were displayed to the public for the first time.[[83]](#cite_note-p1223283570-83)

By early 1938, museum officials sought more than $300,000 for repairs to the museum building,[[84]](#cite_note-p1243640786-84)[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-1938-04-11-85) and then-director Philip Newell Youtz said that parts of the building were crumbling.[[85]](#cite_note-nyt-1938-04-11-85) The [Brooklyn Museum Art School](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum_Art_School "Brooklyn Museum Art School"), formerly a part of the [Brooklyn Academy of Music](/wiki/Brooklyn_Academy_of_Music "Brooklyn Academy of Music"), was moved to the Brooklyn Museum in 1941.[[86]](#cite_note-nyt-1984-12-22-86) An art distribution center sponsored by the [Works Progress Administration](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration") opened on the museum's sixth floor the same year.[[87]](#cite_note-p1320074766-87)[[88]](#cite_note-n128619660-88) The department store chain [Abraham & Straus](/wiki/Abraham_%26_Straus "Abraham & Straus") donated $50,000 in 1948 for the establishment of a "laboratory of industrial design" at the Brooklyn Museum.[[89]](#cite_note-p1564926735-89)[[90]](#cite_note-nyt-1948-04-02-90) By the following year, Brooklyn Institute officials sought to expand the museum as part of a "vast cultural program".[[91]](#cite_note-n128621219-91)[[92]](#cite_note-nyt-1949-10-09-92) The plans involved an annex with a 2,500-seat auditorium behind the west wing, which was planned to cost $500,000, as well as a general renovation of existing facilities, which was to cost $1.5 million.[[92]](#cite_note-nyt-1949-10-09-92) A new 400-seat lecture hall opened at the museum that September, within space formerly occupied by two Egyptian galleries.[[93]](#cite_note-nyt-1949-09-07-93) To attract visitors, the museum expanded its educational programs greatly in the late 1940s.[[94]](#cite_note-nyt-1951-02-09-94)

#### 1950s and 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1950s and 1960s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden_New_York_May_2015_002.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden_New_York_May_2015_002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden_New_York_May_2015_002.jpg)

Rear of the museum building, expanded in the 1950s

Brooklyn Institute officials announced plans in 1951 to repair the Brooklyn Museum as part of the institute's long-term plan to convert the museum into a cultural center.[[95]](#cite_note-n8922607-95) The museum's Egyptian galleries began undergoing renovations the same year.[[96]](#cite_note-p1324187503-96)[[97]](#cite_note-nyt-1952-01-06-97) The renovation of the Egyptian galleries, the first phase of the museum's $3.5 million overhaul, was finished in November 1953.[[98]](#cite_note-n128697395-98)[[99]](#cite_note-nyt-1953-11-17-99) Brown, Lawford & Forbes designed a rear annex for the museum in 1955.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) The museum's furniture, sculpture, and watercolor galleries reopened in 1957 following the second stage of the renovation.[[100]](#cite_note-nyt-1957-02-19-100)[[101]](#cite_note-p1326289458-101) The rear annex contained a new stairway,[[101]](#cite_note-p1326289458-101) which led to new galleries on the fourth through sixth stories of the center section.[[102]](#cite_note-n129431381-102) By the late 1950s, the museum was running low on funds, with director Edgar C. Schenck blaming the museum's fiscal woes on Manhattan residents' unwillingness to cross the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") to visit Brooklyn.[[103]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1958_z105-103) Due to a shortage of security guards, the museum was forced to close some galleries part-time.[[104]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1958_i269-104)

Another Egyptian gallery opened in April 1959,[[105]](#cite_note-nyt-1959-04-14-105)[[106]](#cite_note-106) and a "pattern library" for teaching opened that July.[[107]](#cite_note-p1327054968-107)[[108]](#cite_note-p1565234557-108) A continued shortage of security guards forced the Brooklyn Museum to close two days a week at the beginning of 1961;[[109]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1960_i358-109) the museum went back to seven-day operations in June 1961 after the city provided money for additional guards.[[110]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1961_w038-110) To attract visitors, the museum began providing a larger variety of programs and adding interactive exhibits and programming.[[111]](#cite_note-p1326285567-111) The Brooklyn Museum announced in 1964 that it would build a special-exhibit gallery on the first floor and an open study/storage gallery on the fifth floor.[[112]](#cite_note-n129432824-112)[[113]](#cite_note-nyt-1964-06-23-113) The Hall of the Americas opened on the museum's first floor the following May.[[114]](#cite_note-n129432484-114)[[115]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1965_p545-115) A sculpture garden, consisting of architectural details salvaged from demolished buildings across New York City, opened at the museum in April 1966.[[116]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1966_h933-116)[[117]](#cite_note-n129433299-117) The Brooklyn Museum and the Metropolitan Museum of Art began coordinating joint programs and exhibitions in 1967.[[118]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1967_n786-118)

By the late 1960s, the museum was again facing a funding shortage; several galleries had been temporarily closed due to a lack of money, and its director Thomas Buechner was considering closing the museum two days a week.[[119]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1968_p720-119) Brooklyn Museum officials also wanted to hire additional security guards to deter crime.[[120]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1969_p185-120) The Brooklyn Museum's Community Gallery, exhibiting black New Yorkers' art, opened in October 1968[[121]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1968_k863-121)[[122]](#cite_note-p226784000-122) following advocacy from Federated Institutes of Cultural Enrichment (FICE), a coalition of Brooklyn-based arts organizations.[[123]](#cite_note-Anagnost_2020-123) The gallery occupied a narrow corridor at ground level.[[124]](#cite_note-p147830601-124) Henri Ghent, the director of the Community Gallery, estimated in 1970 that "perhaps 100,000" additional patrons had been attracted to the museum after the gallery opened,[[124]](#cite_note-p147830601-124)[[125]](#cite_note-nyt-1970-02-04-125) including black patrons who had never before visited a museum.[[125]](#cite_note-nyt-1970-02-04-125)

#### 1970s and early 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1970s and early 1980s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Liberty_at_Brooklyn_Museum_jeh.JPG/250px-Liberty_at_Brooklyn_Museum_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Liberty_at_Brooklyn_Museum_jeh.JPG)

Replica of the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Replicas_of_the_Statue_of_Liberty "Replicas of the Statue of Liberty") (*Liberty Enlightening the World*) in back lot

The Brooklyn Museum continued to experience financial shortfalls in the early 1970s.[[125]](#cite_note-nyt-1970-02-04-125) Due to a shortage of security guards, in mid-1971, museum officials announced that they would close the museum two days per week, allowing all galleries to remain open even with limited security.[[126]](#cite_note-p148166722-126)[[127]](#cite_note-nyt-1971-05-24-127) The museum also reopened its 23 period rooms that October after a yearlong closure,[[128]](#cite_note-n132868952-128) and they also opened a new period room, themed to a private study.[[128]](#cite_note-n132868952-128)[[129]](#cite_note-129) Officials planned to move the Community Gallery to a dedicated space adjoining the museum;[[130]](#cite_note-nyt-1971-09-12-130) the gallery was popular among guests but did not have enough funding from the museum itself.[[131]](#cite_note-p226720450-131) By late 1973, twenty percent of the museum's staff professionals had resigned amid a dispute involving director [Duncan F. Cameron](/w/index.php?title=Duncan_F._Cameron&action=edit&redlink=1 "Duncan F. Cameron (page does not exist)")'s firing of another employee,[[132]](#cite_note-132) eventually prompting Cameron's own resignation that year.[[133]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1973_i686-133)[[134]](#cite_note-p1240163688-134) Further staff disputes complicated the search for a replacement director,[[135]](#cite_note-nyt-1974-05-16-135) and many employees went on strike in 1974 because they wanted to form a labor union.[[136]](#cite_note-n132871383-136)[[137]](#cite_note-137)

By the mid-1970s, there were plans to split the [Brooklyn Children's Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Children%27s_Museum "Brooklyn Children's Museum") and the Brooklyn Museum Art School from the Brooklyn Museum.[[138]](#cite_note-138) At the time, the museum received $1.5 million per year from the city.[[139]](#cite_note-nyt-1973-11-27-139) Four galleries for Korean and Japanese art opened at the museum in October 1974,[[140]](#cite_note-nyt-1974-10-13-140)[[141]](#cite_note-n132872439-141) and the African art galleries reopened in December 1976 following an expansion and renovation.[[142]](#cite_note-p226616695-142)[[143]](#cite_note-p122780877-143) The Brooklyn Museum also began renovating 21 American [period rooms](/wiki/Period_room "Period room") in 1976.[[144]](#cite_note-nyt-1984-10-18-144) Following a 1978 investigation into some of the museum's acquisitions, state attorney general [Louis J. Lefkowitz](/wiki/Louis_J._Lefkowitz "Louis J. Lefkowitz") recommended that the museum implement "a comprehensive code of ethics".[[145]](#cite_note-nyt-1978-06-23-145) The same year, the Brooklyn Museum partnered with [Designgroup](/w/index.php?title=Designgroup&action=edit&redlink=1 "Designgroup (page does not exist)") and the Egyptian government to restore the [Cairo Museum](/wiki/Cairo_Museum "Cairo Museum")'s collection.[[146]](#cite_note-n132919426-146) Due to budget cuts, the Brooklyn Museum eliminated its Middle Eastern art division in 1979,[[147]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1979_p490-147) despite the fact that the museum had frequently applied for federal grants in the preceding years, most of which had been approved from 1976 to 1978.[[148]](#cite_note-148)

Two of the museum's period rooms reopened in 1980 following a renovation.[[149]](#cite_note-nyt-1980-03-23-149) By then, director Michael Botwinick was considering several measures to reduce the museum's budgetary shortfalls, including halving the number of art classes, closing the museum during the workweek, and hosting fewer exhibits per year.[[150]](#cite_note-n132921778-150) At the time, the museum received 31 percent of its funds from the city, a higher percentage than other New York City museums;[[150]](#cite_note-n132921778-150) the city still owned the building itself.[[151]](#cite_note-nyt-1982-11-28-151) After Robert Buck became director in 1983, he began hosting additional art classes, attracting members, and raising money for the museum,[[152]](#cite_note-nyt-1985-01-22-152) which struggled to compete with more famous institutions in Manhattan.[[153]](#cite_note-p2779645052-153) In 1984, the museum completed the renovations of its last period rooms[[144]](#cite_note-nyt-1984-10-18-144)[[154]](#cite_note-n132923147-154) and opened a gallery for "early-19th-century decorative arts".[[154]](#cite_note-n132923147-154) The unprofitable Brooklyn Museum Art School was closed the same year,[[86]](#cite_note-nyt-1984-12-22-86) and the museum obtained $14 million in city funding to upgrade the climate-control systems.[[152]](#cite_note-nyt-1985-01-22-152) The museum resumed Monday operations in late 1984 after receiving additional city funding,[[155]](#cite_note-155) and it started running TV advertisements in 1985.[[156]](#cite_note-nyt-1985-05-22-156)

#### Mid-1980s and 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mid-1980s and 1990s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Museu_do_brooklyn_%2816235126548%29.jpg/250px-Museu_do_brooklyn_%2816235126548%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museu_do_brooklyn_(16235126548).jpg)

An exhibition hall in the museum

The Brooklyn Museum announced a master plan in March 1986.[[157]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-03-18-157)[[158]](#cite_note-n127739716-158) The plan involved doubling the amount of exhibition space in the building from 450,000 to 830,000 square feet (42,000 to 77,000 m2).[[157]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-03-18-157) At the time, the museum could only exhibit about five percent of its collection simultaneously,[[157]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-03-18-157) as its building was one-sixth as large as originally planned.[[159]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-07-20-159) The museum was to expand its storage, classroom, and conservation facilities and add an auditorium.[[157]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-03-18-157) Buck met with the heads of all of the museum's departments to determine how much exhibit and storage space they needed.[[159]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-07-20-159) The museum also planned a new entrance from the Brooklyn Botanic Garden, which had twice as many annual visitors;[[158]](#cite_note-n127739716-158)[[159]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-07-20-159) the Botanic Garden entrance had been planned by McKim, Mead & White but never executed.[[157]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-03-18-157) The project was expected to cost $50 million to $100 million,[[159]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-07-20-159)[[158]](#cite_note-n127739716-158) which was to be funded by the city's capital budget.[[160]](#cite_note-p285377946-160)

Museum officials held an [architectural design competition](/wiki/Architectural_design_competition "Architectural design competition") to redesign the west wing,[[161]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-05-27-161)[[162]](#cite_note-p285504974-162) attracting 103 competitors;[[163]](#cite_note-p219201884-163) they hired [Arata Isozaki](/wiki/Arata_Isozaki "Arata Isozaki") of [James Stewart Polshek Partners](/wiki/James_Stewart_Polshek "James Stewart Polshek") that October.[[164]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-10-17-164)[[165]](#cite_note-p1830721135-165) Isozaki's design retained much of McKim, Mead & White's original plan but included a "great hall" and trapezoidal courtyards,[[165]](#cite_note-p1830721135-165) as well as an angled rear wall and an obelisk.[[163]](#cite_note-p219201884-163) Buck expressed optimism that media coverage of the design competition would attract additional visitors, even if the master plan was never completely carried out.[[166]](#cite_note-p277858017-166) The scope of the renovation grew quickly, with estimated costs reaching $200 million by early 1988.[[167]](#cite_note-p277964505-167) Iris and B. Gerald Cantor donated $3.5 million for the museum's auditorium in 1989,[[168]](#cite_note-nyt-1989-05-04-168) and the city gave another $2 million for other work.[[169]](#cite_note-p277983501-169) The Brooklyn Museum announced in 1990 that it would begin the first phase of renovation, which was to cost $31 million. This involved converting the offices in the west wing to about 64,000 square feet (5,900 m2) of gallery space for its Egyptian collection, as well as building storage space and an auditorium.[[170]](#cite_note-170) The same year, budget cuts prompted museum officials to lay off employees[[171]](#cite_note-nyt-1990-06-23-171) and close its doors on Mondays.[[172]](#cite_note-nyt-1990-12-24-172)

The auditorium opened in 1991; at the time, there had not been an auditorium at the museum for over half a century.[[173]](#cite_note-nyt-1991-04-08-173) About 33,000 square feet (3,100 m2) in the museum's west wing reopened as gallery space in November 1993.[[174]](#cite_note-n127455379-174)[[175]](#cite_note-nyt-1993-11-28-175) The renovation retained the original layout of the west-wing spaces.[[176]](#cite_note-n127455267-176) *The New York Times* described Isozaki and Polshek's renovation as aiming for "clean, serene spaces"; the rooms had rooms with maple floors, white walls, horizontal lighting strips, and granite [baseboards](/wiki/Baseboard "Baseboard").[[177]](#cite_note-nyt-1993-11-30-177) The west wing was renamed for investor [Morris A. Schapiro](/wiki/Morris_Schapiro "Morris Schapiro") and his brother, art historian [Meyer Schapiro](/wiki/Meyer_Schapiro "Meyer Schapiro"), in early 1994 after Morris Schapiro donated $5 million.[[178]](#cite_note-n127743281-178)[[179]](#cite_note-nyt-1994-08-14-179)

The Brooklyn Museum changed its name to **Brooklyn Museum of Art** in 1997.[[180]](#cite_note-nyt-1997-03-17-180) According to acting director Linda S. Ferber, the renaming was necessary because "there was more confusion about the museum's identity than we supposed"; for instance, many visitors still believed the museum had natural-history exhibits, which had not been the case since 1934.[[181]](#cite_note-nyt-1997-01-31-181)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 21st century")]

#### 2000s and 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2000s and 2010s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/BrooklynMuseumJanuary2007.jpg/250px-BrooklynMuseumJanuary2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrooklynMuseumJanuary2007.jpg)

The Eastern Parkway entrance to the Brooklyn Museum, rebuilt in 2004

Brooklyn Museum officials hired architect [James Polshek](/wiki/James_Polshek "James Polshek") in 2000 to design a new glass-clad entrance for the building at a cost of $55 million.[[182]](#cite_note-nyt-2000-09-20-182)[[183]](#cite_note-n127641060-183) Polshek described the front entrance as a "wasteland" at the time, and he said he wanted to build "Brooklyn's new front stoop".[[182]](#cite_note-nyt-2000-09-20-182) The [New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission") approved Polshek's design, despite opposition from preservationists.[[184]](#cite_note-p305880467-184) The renovation cost $63 million[[185]](#cite_note-nyt-2004-04-12-185)[[186]](#cite_note-n127641604-186) and also added air conditioning throughout the museum building.[[187]](#cite_note-187) The [Henry Luce Foundation](/wiki/Henry_Luce "Henry Luce") gave the museum a $10 million grant in 2001, which funded the construction of the Luce Center for American Art on the fifth floor.[[188]](#cite_note-nyt-2001-04-21-188) The museum's renovation was completed in April 2004.[[185]](#cite_note-nyt-2004-04-12-185)[[186]](#cite_note-n127641604-186) At the same time, the museum announced that it would revert to its previous name, Brooklyn Museum.[[189]](#cite_note-muschamp-189)[[190]](#cite_note-Artopia_2004_r620-190) By then, the Brooklyn Museum was focusing on attracting Brooklyn residents, rather than visitors from other boroughs.[[185]](#cite_note-nyt-2004-04-12-185) The [Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art](/wiki/Elizabeth_A._Sackler_Center_for_Feminist_Art "Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art") opened on the museum's fourth floor in March 2007.[[191]](#cite_note-micucci-191)[[192]](#cite_note-Hajela_2007_k717-192)

The museum extensively renovated its Great Hall, which reopened in early 2011,[[193]](#cite_note-Carlson_2011_r679-193)[[194]](#cite_note-nyt-2011-02-24-194) and it relocated and reopened its African art gallery on the first floor the same year.[[195]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2011_f952-195) A 4,150-square-foot (386 m2) museum shop opened at the Brooklyn Museum in early 2012,[[196]](#cite_note-Cameron_2012_m893-196)[[197]](#cite_note-Schuh_2012_k928-197) followed later that year by a new cafe.[[198]](#cite_note-Levine_2013_k091-198) The upscale restaurant [Saul](/wiki/Saul_(restaurant) "Saul (restaurant)") opened within the Brooklyn Museum in October 2013,[[199]](#cite_note-Musumeci_2013_q816-199)[[200]](#cite_note-Casey_2013_o469-200) changing its name to The Norm in 2016.[[201]](#cite_note-nyt-2016-05-17-201)[[202]](#cite_note-Morabito_2016_i310-202) For a decade starting in the mid-2010s, the museum spent $100 million on various upgrades to its building.[[203]](#cite_note-D_Souza_z899-203) By then, the museum was facing financial difficulties, and half of the 465,000 annual patrons did not pay admission because of the museum's [suggested admission](/wiki/Suggested_admission "Suggested admission") policy.[[204]](#cite_note-nyt-2017-04-30-204) The Brooklyn Museum's Chinese-art gallery reopened in 2019.[[205]](#cite_note-NYT_Asian-205)

#### 2020s to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2020s to present")]

The museum was temporarily closed from March to October 2020 because of the [COVID-19 pandemic in New York City](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City").[[206]](#cite_note-nyt-2020-08-19-206) During the [George Floyd protests in New York City](/wiki/George_Floyd_protests_in_New_York_City "George Floyd protests in New York City") in June 2020, the museum participated in the Open Your Lobby initiative, being one of two major art institutions in New York City (along with [MoMA PS1](/wiki/MoMA_PS1 "MoMA PS1")) to provide protesters with shelter or resources.[[207]](#cite_note-Holmes_2020_z563-207) The Brooklyn Museum received $50 million from the New York City government in 2021, the largest such gift in the museum's history.[[208]](#cite_note-nyt-2021-11-22-208)[[209]](#cite_note-Artnet_News_2021_y852-209) The money was to be used to renovate 40,000 square feet (3,700 m2) into gallery space.[[210]](#cite_note-Hickman_2021_x102-210)[[211]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Eagle_2022_n986-211) The museum's South Asian and Islamic galleries reopened in 2022, completing a 12-year renovation of the Asian galleries.[[212]](#cite_note-Herman_2022_u881-212)[[213]](#cite_note-Angeleti_2022_p634-213) To make way for additional exhibition space, in early 2024 the museum sold off 200 objects and the contents of four period rooms.[[214]](#cite_note-Boucher_2024_n829-214) That January, the museum opened its Toby Devan Lewis Education Center, which contains three studios and a gallery.[[215]](#cite_note-215)[[216]](#cite_note-216) The museum started using a new logo that September.[[217]](#cite_note-217)[[218]](#cite_note-218)

In 2021, Brooklyn Museum staff organized to form a union representing various roles, including curators, educators, and front-desk workers.[[219]](#cite_note-Cascone_2023-219) The union vote passed with strong support, and union members ratified their first contract in November 2023, following two years of negotiations.[[219]](#cite_note-Cascone_2023-219)[[220]](#cite_note-220)[[221]](#cite_note-221) Due to increasing financial deficits, in museum officials laid off employees and reduced the number of annual exhibitions in early 2025,[[222]](#cite_note-222)[[223]](#cite_note-223) though further job reductions were averted due to increased funding from the city.[[224]](#cite_note-224) In early 2026, the museum hired [Peterson Rich Office](/w/index.php?title=Peterson_Rich_Office&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peterson Rich Office (page does not exist)") to renovate 6,400 square feet (590 m2) of space on the third floor,[[225]](#cite_note-Roche_f600-225)[[226]](#cite_note-Seaman_m492-226) creating four galleries for the museum's African art collection.[[227]](#cite_note-Benzine_i867-227) The renovation would take a year and cost $13 million.[[225]](#cite_note-Roche_f600-225)[[227]](#cite_note-Benzine_i867-227)

## Building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Building")]

The Brooklyn Museum building is a steel frame structure clad in masonry, designed in the [neoclassical style](/wiki/Neoclassical_style "Neoclassical style") by the [architectural](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture") firm of [McKim, Mead, and White](/wiki/McKim,_Mead,_and_White "McKim, Mead, and White") and built by the Carlin Construction Company.[[228]](#cite_note-aia-228) The original museum building is a [New York City designated landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_designated_landmark "New York City designated landmark")[[164]](#cite_note-nyt-1986-10-17-164) and was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in June 1978.[[229]](#cite_note-n132921414-229) The museum shares a large city block with Brooklyn's [Central Library](/wiki/Central_Library_(Brooklyn_Public_Library) "Central Library (Brooklyn Public Library)"), [Mount Prospect Park](/wiki/Mount_Prospect_Park "Mount Prospect Park"), and the [Brooklyn Botanic Garden](/wiki/Brooklyn_Botanic_Garden "Brooklyn Botanic Garden") to the west and south.[[230]](#cite_note-230)

### Exterior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Exterior")]

The original design for the Brooklyn Museum proposed a structure four times as large as what was built from 1893 through 1927, when construction ended.[[228]](#cite_note-aia-228) As designed, the three-story museum building was supposed to have several wings, centered around a memorial hall and clustered around four [light courts](/wiki/Light_court "Light court").[[10]](#cite_note-p126805239-10)[[231]](#cite_note-p136576182-231) After Brooklyn became part of greater New York City in 1898, support for the project diminished.[[228]](#cite_note-aia-228) Only the wings on the northern end, as well as the northeastern light court (known as the Auditorium Court), were built; the resulting L-shaped building covers a site of about 4.5 acres (1.8 ha).[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2–3 Although additional wings were built behind the original east wing over the years (creating the current light court), nothing was built behind the west wing.[[174]](#cite_note-n127455379-174)[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2 This led the *New York Daily News* to liken the museum building to a movie set.[[174]](#cite_note-n127455379-174)

#### Main facade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Main facade")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Brooklyn_Museum_Night_2015.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_Night_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_Night_2015.jpg)

The museum at night

The primary [elevation](/wiki/Elevation_(architecture) "Elevation (architecture)") of the facade, facing north along Eastern Parkway, is 510 feet (160 m) wide and consists of the west and east wings, which flank a projecting pavilion with a [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico"). Additional pavilions project from the facade at either end. The center portico contains six [Ionic](/wiki/Ionic_order "Ionic order") columns that support a [pediment](/wiki/Pediment "Pediment") with sculptures in its [tympanum](/wiki/Tympanum_(architecture) "Tympanum (architecture)").[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2 The portico was originally accessed by a staircase that was removed in 1936–1937.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 3[[233]](#cite_note-NYCL-0155-233) [Daniel Chester French](/wiki/Daniel_Chester_French "Daniel Chester French") was responsible for the pediment sculptures. French also designed the two allegorical figures *Brooklyn* and *Manhattan* flanking the museum's entrance; they were created in 1916 for the Brooklyn approach to the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") and relocated to the museum in 1963.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 3[[234]](#cite_note-234) Above the pediment is a copper cresting with [anthemia](/wiki/Anthemion "Anthemion"), as well as a low [saucer dome](/wiki/Saucer_dome "Saucer dome").[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2[[233]](#cite_note-NYCL-0155-233) The modern main entrance, dating to Polshek's 2004 renovation, consists of a glass pavilion with four metal pylons, as well as a semicircular plaza just outside.[[189]](#cite_note-muschamp-189)[[184]](#cite_note-p305880467-184) A set of brick [piers](/wiki/Pier_(architecture) "Pier (architecture)"), which had supported the original entrance staircase, was repurposed into a brick arch in 2004.[[235]](#cite_note-n127696103-235)

The pavilions at either end of the Eastern Parkway facade protrude only slightly from the facade and contain engaged columns in the Ionic order. The west and east wings are divided vertically by [pilasters](/wiki/Pilaster "Pilaster"); between each set of pilasters are windows with [architraves](/wiki/Architrave "Architrave"). The [entablature](/wiki/Entablature "Entablature") above the pilasters contains a frieze with inscribed names of figures who represent knowledge.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2

The Eastern Parkway facade is topped by 20 monolithic figures on the cornice: one above each pilaster on the west and east wings, and four above the pavilions.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2 An additional ten figures, five each on the western and eastern elevations of the outermost pavilions, were sculpted.[[236]](#cite_note-p572018007-236) The sculptures were carved by the [Piccirilli Brothers](/wiki/Piccirilli_Brothers "Piccirilli Brothers"), who sculpted a total of 30 figures on the museum's facade.[[237]](#cite_note-nyt-1999-10-17-237)[[238]](#cite_note-Tolles_Dimmick_Hassler_1999_p._482-238) Fourteen sculptors were hired to design the sculptures, which each measure 12 feet (3.7 m) high. Had the full building been completed, there would have been 80 sculptures in total, with 20 each depicting classical subjects, medieval and Renaissance subjects, modern European and American subjects, and Asian subjects. The 30 extant sculptures consist of the 20 classical sculptures (10 Greek and 10 Roman) on the northern elevation, as well as five Persian and five Chinese sculptures on the side elevations.[[236]](#cite_note-p572018007-236)

#### Other facades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Other facades")]

The eastern elevation of the facade faces Washington Avenue, where only the pavilion at the northern end was built. The rest of the eastern elevation is similar to that on Eastern Parkway, with pilasters dividing it vertically into seven [bays](/wiki/Bay_(architecture) "Bay (architecture)"). Unlike on Eastern Parkway, the pilasters are topped by shorter pilasters rather than sculptures.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 2 The southern elevation faces a parking lot and contains a masonry facade and some windows.[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 3 There is also an annex to the south, designed by Brown, Lawford & Forbes, which contains a secondary entrance and a stairway.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7)[[232]](#cite_note-NRHP-77000944-232): 3

### Interior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Interior")]

The oldest portion of the building measured 193 by 71 feet (59 by 22 m) and comprised only about three percent of what was originally planned. The center of the first floor would have contained a memorial hall, while a "great hall of sculpture" would have extended to the north and south of the memorial hall. To the west of the memorial hall would have been gallery space for artwork on loan, while to the east would have been a multi-story auditorium. The remaining corners of the first floor would have included several additional galleries for the museum's permanent collections, and the light courts would have exhibited large objects. The second floor would have housed more collections and lecture rooms, while the third floor would have had the library, music room, and galleries for images, domestic art, and science. An additional story, above the central part of the building, would have housed more departments of the museum.[[10]](#cite_note-p126805239-10)

The main lobby, originally occupied by the ground-level auditorium, was built during the mid-20th century as a modern-style space.[[239]](#cite_note-n127454116-239) Although then-director Philip Newell Youtz was the [architect of record](/wiki/Architect_of_record "Architect of record"), the lobby's design may have been influenced by [William Lescaze](/wiki/William_Lescaze "William Lescaze"), who was Youtz's friend.[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) The lobby, containing black-glass panels and indirect lighting, was described in the 1939 *[WPA Guide to New York City](/wiki/WPA_Guide "WPA Guide")* as "an example of the best in modern architecture... devoid of the elaborate decoration which so often clutters up the entrances of public building."[[7]](#cite_note-nyt-1987-10-11-7) Following a 2011 renovation, the lobby was redesigned as a double-height central gallery surrounded by 25-foot-tall (7.6 m) columns.[[193]](#cite_note-Carlson_2011_r679-193)[[194]](#cite_note-nyt-2011-02-24-194)

## Operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Operations")]

The Brooklyn Museum is operated by a nonprofit of the same name, which was established in 1935.[[240]](#cite_note-GuideStar_Profile_b475-240) The museum is part of the [Cultural Institutions Group](/wiki/Cultural_Institutions_Group "Cultural Institutions Group") (CIG), a group of institutions that occupy land or buildings owned by the New York City government and derive part of their yearly funding from the city.[[241]](#cite_note-Welcome_to_NYC.gov_d849-241) It was also part of the [Brooklyn Educational Cultural Alliance](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Educational_Cultural_Alliance&action=edit&redlink=1 "Brooklyn Educational Cultural Alliance (page does not exist)") during the late 20th century.[[242]](#cite_note-nyt-1983-12-18-242) During the late 1980s, the museum was part of a group called Destination Brooklyn, which sought to attract visitors to Brooklyn;[[243]](#cite_note-p278140120-243) this initiative had stalled by the early 1990s.[[244]](#cite_note-244)

### Directors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Directors")]

[Franklin Hooper](/wiki/Franklin_Hooper "Franklin Hooper") was the Brooklyn Institute's first director, serving for 25 years until his death in 1914.[[245]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1914_z476-245) Hooper was succeeded by William Henry Fox, who served from 1914 to his retirement in 1934.[[246]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1934_y532-246)[[247]](#cite_note-n127453619-247) Fox was followed by Philip Newell Youtz from 1934 to 1938.[[248]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1938_p737-248)[[249]](#cite_note-p1242893420-249) Laurance Page Roberts was director from 1938 to 1942, when his wife Isabel Spaulding Roberts became interim director on his behalf;[[250]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2002_t562-250) L. P. Roberts formally resigned in 1946.[[251]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1946_c719-251)[[252]](#cite_note-p1291247273-252) His immediate successor, Charles Nagel Jr., served for nine years until he resigned in 1955.[[253]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1954_l251-253) Edgar Craig Schenck, who was appointed director shortly afterward,[[254]](#cite_note-nyt-1954-12-18-254) served until his death in 1959.[[255]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1959_f820-255)[[256]](#cite_note-p1324101661-256) [Thomas S. Buechner](/wiki/Thomas_S._Buechner "Thomas S. Buechner") became the museum's director in 1960,[[257]](#cite_note-p1326076848-257)[[258]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1960_a048-258) making him one of the youngest directors in the country.[[259]](#cite_note-Grimes-259) During Buechner's tenure, [Donelson Hoopes](/wiki/Donelson_Hoopes "Donelson Hoopes") was hired as Curator of Paintings and Sculptures from 1965 to 1969.[[260]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2006_i466-260)

Duncan F. Cameron assumed the directorship in 1971, following Buechner's resignation;[[261]](#cite_note-261) Cameron himself resigned in 1973.[[133]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1973_i686-133)[[134]](#cite_note-p1240163688-134) Michael Kan was appointed as acting director in early 1974,[[262]](#cite_note-262) serving for a few months.[[135]](#cite_note-nyt-1974-05-16-135) He was succeeded by Michael Botwinick, who was appointed in 1974[[263]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1974_r554-263) and stepped down in 1982.[[264]](#cite_note-264) Robert T. Buck became director in 1983[[265]](#cite_note-nyt-1983-02-19a-265) and served until he resigned in 1996, upon which Linda S. Ferber became acting director.[[266]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1996_y791-266) Arnold L. Lehman was named as the museum's director in April 1997,[[267]](#cite_note-267) and Lehman announced in September 2014 that he would retire the next year.[[268]](#cite_note-268) In May 2015, [Creative Time](/wiki/Creative_Time "Creative Time") president and artistic director [Anne Pasternak](/wiki/Anne_Pasternak "Anne Pasternak") was named the museum's next director;[[269]](#cite_note-269) she assumed the position on September 1, 2015.[[270]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_2015_t867-270)

Since 2014, the director's position has formally been known as the *Shelby White and Leon Levy Director of the Brooklyn Museum*, after [Leon Levy Foundation](/wiki/Leon_Levy_Foundation "Leon Levy Foundation") cofounder [Shelby White](/wiki/Shelby_White "Shelby White") donated $5 million to the directorship's endowment.[[271]](#cite_note-Artnet_News_2014_i295-271)[[272]](#cite_note-Kozinn_2014_o592-272)

### Funding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Funding")]

According to the museum's website, it receives funding from the [city government](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City"), Brooklyn [borough president](/wiki/Borough_president "Borough president")'s office, mayor's office, [New York City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council"), [state government](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_(state) "Government of New York (state)"), [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_States "Government of the United States"), and other agencies.[[273]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Public_Support-273) In 2005, the museum was among 406 New York City arts and social service institutions to receive part of a $20 million grant from the [Carnegie Corporation](/wiki/Carnegie_Corporation "Carnegie Corporation"), in turn funded by New York City mayor [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg").[[274]](#cite_note-roberts-274)[[275]](#cite_note-carnegie-275) Major benefactors have historically included [Frank Lusk Babbott](/wiki/Frank_Lusk_Babbott "Frank Lusk Babbott"). The museum is the site of the annual Brooklyn Artists Ball which has included celebrity hosts such as [Sarah Jessica Parker](/wiki/Sarah_Jessica_Parker "Sarah Jessica Parker") and [Liv Tyler](/wiki/Liv_Tyler "Liv Tyler").[[276]](#cite_note-ball-276)

Prior to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the Brooklyn Museum had an endowment of $108 million, but the museum applied for federal funding through the [Paycheck Protection Program](/wiki/Paycheck_Protection_Program "Paycheck Protection Program") after its endowment declined by one-fifth in 2020.[[277]](#cite_note-Small_Small_2020_a335-277) Amid the pandemic and its [negative impact](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_arts_and_cultural_heritage "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the arts and cultural heritage") on museum revenue, the museum raised funds for an endowment to pay for collections care by selling or [deaccessioning](/wiki/Deaccessioning "Deaccessioning") works of art. The October 2020 sale consisted of 12 works by artists including [Lucas Cranach the Elder](/wiki/Lucas_Cranach_the_Elder "Lucas Cranach the Elder"), [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet"), and [Jean-Baptiste-Camille Corot](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste-Camille_Corot "Jean-Baptiste-Camille Corot"),[[278]](#cite_note-Pogrebin-278) while other sales throughout that month included Modernist artists.[[279]](#cite_note-279) Though usually prohibited by the [Association of Art Museum Directors](/wiki/Association_of_Art_Museum_Directors "Association of Art Museum Directors"), the association allowed such sales to proceed for a two-year window through 2022 in response to the effects of the pandemic.[[280]](#cite_note-280)

## Art and exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Art and exhibitions")]

The Brooklyn Museum's collection contains around 500,000 objects.[[2]](#cite_note-nyt-2021-11-222-2) In the twentieth century, Brooklyn Museum exhibitions sought to present an encyclopedic view of art and culture, with a focus on educating a broad public.[[3]](#cite_note-Lawrence_1987-3) By the 21st century, the Brooklyn Museum frequently hosted shows that promoted cultural diversity and, in particular, underrepresented demographic groups.[[281]](#cite_note-nyt-2024-06-14-281)

### Notable exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Notable exhibitions")]

In 1923, the museum was one of the first U.S. institutions to exhibit African cast-metal and other objects as art, rather than as ethnological artifacts.[[282]](#cite_note-282)[[283]](#cite_note-283) The museum's acquisitions during this time also included such varied objects as the interior of a Swiss house,[[284]](#cite_note-p1112749912-284) a stained glass window,[[285]](#cite_note-p1111998929-285) and a pipe organ.[[286]](#cite_note-p1111983757-286) The museum's first period room opened in 1929; these period rooms represented middle-class and non-elite citizens' homes, in contrast to other museums. which tended to focus on upper-class period rooms.[[287]](#cite_note-287) The 17th-century [Jans Martense Schenck house](/wiki/Jans_Martense_Schenck_house "Jans Martense Schenck house") became part of the Brooklyn Museum's collection in the 1950s,[[288]](#cite_note-nyt-1950-04-17-288) as did the interior of a room in [John D. Rockefeller Jr.](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller_Jr. "John D. Rockefeller Jr.")'s [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") home.[[289]](#cite_note-289)

In 1967 the Federated Institutes of Cultural Enrichment (FICE), a coalition of Brooklyn-based arts organizations, demanded that the Brooklyn Museum exhibit more works by artists from the borough, especially African American artists.[[123]](#cite_note-Anagnost_2020-123)[[290]](#cite_note-290)[[291]](#cite_note-291) The museum then hired black curator Henri Ghent to direct a new "Community Gallery", supported at first by the [New York State Council on the Arts](/wiki/New_York_State_Council_on_the_Arts "New York State Council on the Arts");[[123]](#cite_note-Anagnost_2020-123) he worked at the museum till 1972.[[292]](#cite_note-nyt-1972-09-15-292) Ghent's first exhibition, *Contemporary Afro-American Arts* (1968), included artists [Joe Overstreet](/wiki/Joe_Overstreet "Joe Overstreet"), [Kay Brown](/wiki/Kay_Brown_(artist) "Kay Brown (artist)"), Frank Smith, and [Otto Neals](/wiki/Otto_Neals "Otto Neals").[[124]](#cite_note-p147830601-124)[[293]](#cite_note-293)

In 1999–2000, the [Sensation](/wiki/Sensation_(art_exhibition) "Sensation (art exhibition)") exhibition of [Charles Saatchi](/wiki/Charles_Saatchi "Charles Saatchi")'s collection provoked controversy for its inclusion of works such as [Chris Ofili](/wiki/Chris_Ofili "Chris Ofili")'s [The Holy Virgin Mary](/wiki/The_Holy_Virgin_Mary "The Holy Virgin Mary").[[294]](#cite_note-294) The exhibition prompted then-mayor [Rudolph Giuliani](/wiki/Rudolph_Giuliani "Rudolph Giuliani") to threaten to withhold city funding from the museum.[[295]](#cite_note-nyt-1999-09-29-295) In the resulting lawsuit,[[296]](#cite_note-296) a U.S. district court judge ruled that the New York City government could not withhold city funds from the Brooklyn Museum on [First Amendment](/wiki/First_Amendment "First Amendment") grounds.[[297]](#cite_note-p279294754-297)

In 2002, the museum received the work *[The Dinner Party](/wiki/The_Dinner_Party "The Dinner Party")*, by feminist artist [Judy Chicago](/wiki/Judy_Chicago "Judy Chicago"), as a gift from The Elizabeth A. Sackler Foundation. Its permanent exhibition began in 2007, as a centerpiece for the museum's [Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art](/wiki/Elizabeth_A._Sackler_Center_for_Feminist_Art "Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art"). In 2004, the Brooklyn Museum featured *Manifest Destiny*, an 8-by-24-foot (2.4 m × 7.3 m) oil-on-wood mural by [Alexis Rockman](/wiki/Alexis_Rockman "Alexis Rockman") that was commissioned by the museum as a centerpiece for the second-floor Mezzanine Gallery and marked the opening of the museum's renovated Grand Lobby and plaza.[[298]](#cite_note-yablonsky-298)[[299]](#cite_note-rockman-299) Other exhibitions have showcased the works of various contemporary artists including [Patrick Kelly](/wiki/Patrick_Kelly_(fashion_designer) "Patrick Kelly (fashion designer)"), [Chuck Close](/wiki/Chuck_Close "Chuck Close"), [Denis Peterson](/wiki/Denis_Peterson "Denis Peterson"), [Ron Mueck](/wiki/Ron_Mueck "Ron Mueck"), [Takashi Murakami](/wiki/Takashi_Murakami "Takashi Murakami"), [Mat Benote](/wiki/Mat_Benote "Mat Benote"),[[300]](#cite_note-hangin-300) [Kiki Smith](/wiki/Kiki_Smith "Kiki Smith"), [Jim Dine](/wiki/Jim_Dine "Jim Dine"), [Robert Rauschenberg](/wiki/Robert_Rauschenberg "Robert Rauschenberg"), [Ching Ho Cheng](/wiki/Ching_Ho_Cheng "Ching Ho Cheng"), [Sylvia Sleigh](/wiki/Sylvia_Sleigh "Sylvia Sleigh") [William Wegman](/wiki/William_Wegman_(photographer) "William Wegman (photographer)"), [Jimmy de Sana](/wiki/Jimmy_De_Sana "Jimmy De Sana"), [Oscar yi Hou](/wiki/Oscar_yi_Hou "Oscar yi Hou"), [Baseera Khan](/wiki/Baseera_Khan "Baseera Khan"), [Loraine O'Grady](/wiki/Lorraine_O%27Grady "Lorraine O'Grady"), [John Edmonds](/wiki/John_Edmonds_(artist) "John Edmonds (artist)"), [Cecilia Vicuña](/wiki/Cecilia_Vicu%C3%B1a "Cecilia Vicuña"), [Sonia Guiñansaca](/wiki/Sonia_Gui%C3%B1ansaca "Sonia Guiñansaca"), and a 2004 survey show of work by Brooklyn artists, *Open House: Working in Brooklyn*[[301]](#cite_note-openhouse-301)

In 2008, curator Edna Russman announced that she believes 10 out of 30 works of [Coptic art](/wiki/Coptic_art "Coptic art") held in the museum's collection—second-largest in North America are fake. The artworks were exhibited starting in 2009.[[302]](#cite_note-russmanadmits-302)

In early to mid-2018, the museum hosted what was the final stop of the touring exhibit *[David Bowie Is](/wiki/David_Bowie_Is "David Bowie Is")*, which had begun in 2013 in London and visited nearly a dozen countries before reaching the Brooklyn Museum.[[303]](#cite_note-MSN1-303) Costumes from *[The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown_(TV_series) "The Crown (TV series)")* and *[The Queen's Gambit](/wiki/The_Queen%27s_Gambit_(miniseries) "The Queen's Gambit (miniseries)")* television series were put on display as part of its virtual exhibition *"The Queen and the Crown"* in November 2020.[[304]](#cite_note-304)[[305]](#cite_note-305) From June through September 2023, coinciding with the fiftieth anniversary of [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso")'s death, the museum hosted *It's Pablo-matic: Picasso According to Hannah Gadsby*, curated by [Hannah Gadsby](/wiki/Hannah_Gadsby "Hannah Gadsby");[[306]](#cite_note-BM-Pablo-306)[[307]](#cite_note-307) though the exhibition was popular, it was also highly controversial.[[308]](#cite_note-308) To celebrate the 200th anniversary of the Brooklyn Apprentices' Library's incorporation, the museum launched a series of special exhibits and events in 2024.[[309]](#cite_note-Loos_2024_e466-309)

## Collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Collections")]

### Egyptian, Classical, and Ancient Near Eastern Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Egyptian, Classical, and Ancient Near Eastern Art")]

The Brooklyn Museum has been building a collection of [Egyptian](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") artifacts since the beginning of the twentieth century, incorporating both collections purchased from others, such as that of American [Egyptologist](/wiki/Egyptology "Egyptology") [Charles Edwin Wilbour](/wiki/Charles_Edwin_Wilbour "Charles Edwin Wilbour"), whose heirs also donated his library to become the museum's Wilbour Library of Egyptology, and objects obtained during museum-sponsored archeological excavations. The Egyptian collection includes objects ranging from statuary, such as the well-known "Bird Lady" [terra cotta](/wiki/Terra_cotta "Terra cotta") figure, to [papyrus](/wiki/Papyrus "Papyrus") documents (among others the [Brooklyn Papyrus](/wiki/Brooklyn_Papyrus "Brooklyn Papyrus")).[[310]](#cite_note-EgyptColHist-310) The museum has nine mummified Egyptians.[[311]](#cite_note-LoosMelvin-311)

The Egyptian, Classical, and Ancient Near Eastern collections are housed in a series of galleries in the museum. Egyptian artifacts can be found in the long-term exhibit, *Egypt Reborn: Art for Eternity*, as well as in the Martha A. and Robert S. Rubin Galleries. Near Eastern artifacts are located in the [Hagop Kevorkian](/wiki/Hagop_Kevorkian "Hagop Kevorkian") Gallery.[[310]](#cite_note-EgyptColHist-310)

### American art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: American art")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Gilbert_Stuart_-_George_Washington_-_Google_Art_Project_%286966745%29.jpg/250px-Gilbert_Stuart_-_George_Washington_-_Google_Art_Project_%286966745%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilbert_Stuart_-_George_Washington_-_Google_Art_Project_(6966745).jpg)

[Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart"), *Portrait of [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington")*, 1796

[Francis Guy](/wiki/Francis_Guy "Francis Guy")'s *Winter Scene in Brooklyn* (c. 1820[[312]](#cite_note-312)) was the first object in the museum's collection of American art, bequeathed in 1846. In 1855, the museum officially designated a collection of American Art, with the first work commissioned for the collection being a landscape painting by [Asher B. Durand](/wiki/Asher_B._Durand "Asher B. Durand"). Items in the American Art collection include portraits, pastels, sculptures, and prints; all items in the collection date to between c. 1720 and c. 1945.

Represented in the American art collection are works by artists such as [William Edmondson](/wiki/William_Edmondson "William Edmondson") (*Angel*, date unknown), [John Singer Sargent](/wiki/John_Singer_Sargent "John Singer Sargent")'s *[Paul César Helleu](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9sar_Helleu "Paul César Helleu") sketching his wife Alice Guérin* (ca. 1889); [Georgia O'Keeffe](/wiki/Georgia_O%27Keeffe "Georgia O'Keeffe")'s *Dark Tree Trunks* (ca. 1946), and [Winslow Homer](/wiki/Winslow_Homer "Winslow Homer")'s *[Eight Bells](/wiki/Eight_Bells_(painting) "Eight Bells (painting)")* (ca. 1887). Among the most famous works in the collection are [Gilbert Stuart](/wiki/Gilbert_Stuart "Gilbert Stuart")'s portrait of [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and [Edward Hicks](/wiki/Edward_Hicks "Edward Hicks")'s [*The Peaceable Kingdom*](/wiki/Edward_Hicks "Edward Hicks"). The museum also holds a collection by [Emil Fuchs](/wiki/Emil_Fuchs_(artist) "Emil Fuchs (artist)").[[313]](#cite_note-fuchs-313)

Works from the American art collection can be found in various areas of the museum, including in the Steinberg Family Sculpture Garden and in the exhibit, *American Identities: A New Look*, which is contained within the museum's *Visible Storage ▪ Study Center*.[[314]](#cite_note-AmColHist-314) In total, there are approximately 2,000 American Art objects held in storage.[[315]](#cite_note-AmColNo-315)

### Asian art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Asian art")]

In 2019, the museum reopened its Japanese and Chinese exhibits, after reinstalling its Korean section in 2017.[[205]](#cite_note-NYT_Asian-205) The Chinese section offers pieces from more than 5,000 years of Chinese art and shows contemporary pieces on a regular schedule.[[205]](#cite_note-NYT_Asian-205) The Japanese gallery, with its 7,000 pieces, is the largest of the museum's Asian collection and is known for its works from the [Ainu people](/wiki/Ainu_people "Ainu people").[[316]](#cite_note-316) The museum also possesses a special edition of *[One Hundred Famous Views of Edo](/wiki/One_Hundred_Famous_Views_of_Edo "One Hundred Famous Views of Edo")* that incorporates special metallic dust. It was donated to the museum in the 1930s, unbound in the 1970s, put on display in 2000, and then displayed again in 2024.[[317]](#cite_note-317) The museum is also home to works from Bhutan, India, Nepal, Pakistan and southeast Asia.[[318]](#cite_note-318)

### Arts of Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Arts of Africa")]

The oldest acquisitions in the African art collection were collected by the museum in 1900, shortly after the museum's founding.[[319]](#cite_note-p1239979288-319) The collection was expanded in 1922 with items originating largely in what is now the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo"). The next year, the museum hosted one of the first exhibitions of African art in the United States,[[320]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Siegmann_Adandé_Dumouchelle_2009_p.-320) when [Stewart Culin](/wiki/Stewart_Culin "Stewart Culin") of the museum's new Department of Ethnology started displaying 1,400 pieces.[[227]](#cite_note-Benzine_i867-227)

The Brooklyn Museum's African art collection includes more than 6,000 objects.[[321]](#cite_note-321) The African art collection covers 2,500 years of human history and includes sculpture, jewelry, masks, and religious artifacts from more than 100 African cultures. Noteworthy items in this collection include a carved *ndop* figure of a Kuba king, believed to be among the oldest extant *ndop* carvings, and a Lulua mother-and-child figure.[[322]](#cite_note-AfricanColHist-322)

In 2018, the museum drew criticism from groups including [Decolonize This Place](/wiki/Decolonize_This_Place "Decolonize This Place") for its hiring of a white woman as Consulting Curator of African Arts.[[323]](#cite_note-323)[[324]](#cite_note-324) As part of the 2026–2027 renovation of the African art galleries, the art was to be rearranged based on the geographical regions from which they originated.[[203]](#cite_note-D_Souza_z899-203)

### Arts of the Pacific Islands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Arts of the Pacific Islands")]

The museum's collection of Pacific Islands art began in 1900 with the acquisition of 100 wooden figures and [shadow puppets](/wiki/Shadow_play "Shadow play") from [New Guinea](/wiki/New_Guinea "New Guinea") and the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") (now [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")); since that base, the collection has grown to encompass close to 5,000 works. Art in this collection is sourced to numerous Pacific and Indian Ocean islands including [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") and New Zealand, as well as less-populous islands such as [Rapa Nui](/wiki/Rapa_Nui "Rapa Nui") and [Vanuatu](/wiki/Vanuatu "Vanuatu"). Many of the [Marquesan](/wiki/Marquesas_Islands "Marquesas Islands") items in the collection were acquired by the museum from famed Norwegian explorer [Thor Heyerdahl](/wiki/Thor_Heyerdahl "Thor Heyerdahl").[[325]](#cite_note-PacColHist-325)

Art objects in this collection are crafted from a wide variety of materials. The museum lists "coconut fiber, feathers, shells, clay, bone, human hair, wood, moss, and spider webs"[[325]](#cite_note-PacColHist-325) as among the materials used to make artworks that include masks, [tapa cloths](/wiki/Tapa_cloth "Tapa cloth"), sculpture, and jewelry.

### Arts of the Islamic world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Arts of the Islamic world")]

The museum also has art objects and historical texts produced by Muslim artists or about Muslim figures and cultures.[[326]](#cite_note-IslamicCol-326)

### Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art")]

Main article: [Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art](/wiki/Elizabeth_A._Sackler_Center_for_Feminist_Art "Elizabeth A. Sackler Center for Feminist Art")

The museum's center for [feminist art](/wiki/Feminist_art "Feminist art") opened in 2007.[[328]](#cite_note-Newspaper_2007_s444-328)[[192]](#cite_note-Hajela_2007_k717-192) Spanning 8,300 square feet (770 m2),[[192]](#cite_note-Hajela_2007_k717-192) it is dedicated to preserving the history of the movement since the late 20th century, as well as raising awareness of feminist contributions to art, and informing the future of this area of artistic dialogue. Along with an exhibition space and library, the center features a gallery housing a masterwork by [Judy Chicago](/wiki/Judy_Chicago "Judy Chicago"), a large installation called *[The Dinner Party](/wiki/The_Dinner_Party "The Dinner Party")* (1974–1979).[[191]](#cite_note-micucci-191)

### European art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: European art")]

The Brooklyn Museum has among others late Gothic and Early [Italian Renaissance](/wiki/Italian_Renaissance "Italian Renaissance") paintings by [Lorenzo di Niccolo](/wiki/Lorenzo_di_Niccolo "Lorenzo di Niccolo") ("Scenes from the life of Saint Lawrence"), [Sano di Pietro](/wiki/Sano_di_Pietro "Sano di Pietro"), [Nardo di Cione](/wiki/Nardo_di_Cione "Nardo di Cione"), [Lorenzo Monaco](/wiki/Lorenzo_Monaco "Lorenzo Monaco"), Donato de' Bardi ("Saint Jerome"), [Giovanni Bellini](/wiki/Giovanni_Bellini "Giovanni Bellini"). It has Dutch paintings by [Frans Hals](/wiki/Frans_Hals "Frans Hals"), [Gerard Dou](/wiki/Gerard_Dou "Gerard Dou"), and [Thomas de Keyser](/wiki/Thomas_de_Keyser "Thomas de Keyser") as well as others. It has 19th-century French paintings by [Charles Daubigny](/wiki/Charles_Daubigny "Charles Daubigny"), [Narcisse Virgilio Díaz](/wiki/Narcisse_Virgilio_D%C3%ADaz "Narcisse Virgilio Díaz"), [Eugène Boudin](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Boudin "Eugène Boudin") ("Port, Le Havre"), [Berthe Morisot](/wiki/Berthe_Morisot "Berthe Morisot"), [Edgar Degas](/wiki/Edgar_Degas "Edgar Degas"), [Gustave Caillebotte](/wiki/Gustave_Caillebotte "Gustave Caillebotte") ("Railway Bridge at Argenteuil"), [Claude Monet](/wiki/Claude_Monet "Claude Monet") ("Doges Palace, Venice"), the French sculptor [Alfred Barye](/wiki/Alfred_Barye "Alfred Barye"), [Camille Pissarro](/wiki/Camille_Pissarro "Camille Pissarro"), and [Paul Cézanne](/wiki/Paul_C%C3%A9zanne "Paul Cézanne") as well as many others.
