### Other collections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Other collections")]

The museum's costume collection was created in 1946,[[329]](#cite_note-nyt-2008-12-15-329) and the Textile and Costume Collection was unveiled in 1977.[[330]](#cite_note-330) The collection, composed of American and European attire, was described by *The New York Times* as "one of the best in the world".[[329]](#cite_note-nyt-2008-12-15-329) Removed from public display in 1991,[[331]](#cite_note-Carlson_2008_z215-331) the collection was transferred to the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art")'s [Costume Institute](/wiki/Anna_Wintour_Costume_Center "Anna Wintour Costume Center") in 2008.[[329]](#cite_note-nyt-2008-12-15-329)[[332]](#cite_note-Zeke2008-332)

The Brooklyn Museum has had a photography collection since the 19th century. The museum initially did not seek out photographs for its collection, which was initially composed exclusively of photographers' and collectors' gifts.[[333]](#cite_note-nyt-1984-08-05-333) Since 1993, the collection has been part of the Department of Prints, Drawings, and Photographs.[[334]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Photography-334)

The museum's Steinberg Family Sculpture Garden has salvaged architectural elements from throughout New York City.[[335]](#cite_note-Spelling-335) The sculpture garden dates from 1966[[116]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1966_h933-116)[[117]](#cite_note-n129433299-117) and includes objects such as the [Bayard–Condict Building](/wiki/Bayard%E2%80%93Condict_Building "Bayard–Condict Building")'s [capitals](/wiki/Capital_(architecture) "Capital (architecture)") and the sculptures at the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge")'s Brooklyn entrance.[[259]](#cite_note-Grimes-259)

## Libraries and archives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Libraries and archives")]

Main article: [Brooklyn Museum Libraries and Archives](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum_Libraries_and_Archives "Brooklyn Museum Libraries and Archives")

The Brooklyn Museum Libraries and Archives hold approximately 300,000 volumes and over 3,200 feet (980 m) of archives. The collection began in 1823 and is housed in facilities that underwent renovations in 1965, 1984 and 2014.[[336]](#cite_note-index-336)[[337]](#cite_note-collection-library-337)[[338]](#cite_note-renovatedlibrary-338)

## Programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Programs")]

The first Saturday of each month, the Brooklyn Museum stays open until 11 pm, and general admission is waived after 5 pm, although some ticketed exhibitions may require an entrance fee. Regular first Saturday activities include educational family-oriented activities such as collection-based art workshops, gallery tours, lectures, live performances dance parties.[[339]](#cite_note-FirstSat-339) The museum started hosting First Saturdays in October 1998,[[340]](#cite_note-nyt-1999-03-21-340) and the event had attracted 1.5 million total visitors as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit).[[341]](#cite_note-nyt-2023-02-03-341)

As part of the Museum Apprentice Program, the museum hires teenage high schoolers to give tours in the museum's galleries during the summer, assist with the museum's weekend family programs throughout the year, participate in talks with museum [curators](/wiki/Curator "Curator"), serve as a teen advisory board to the museum, and help plan teen events.[[342]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Apprentices-342) The museum also runs the Museum Education Fellowship Program, a ten-month position where fellows lead school group visits with a focus on various topics from the collection.[[343]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Fellows-343) School Youth and Family Fellows teach Gallery Studio Programs and School Partnerships while Adult and Public Programs Fellows curate and organize Thursday night as well as First Saturday Programming.[[343]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_Fellows-343)

The museum has posted many pieces to a digital collection that allows the public to [tag](/wiki/Folksonomy "Folksonomy") and curate sets of objects online, as well as solicit additional scholarship contributions.[[344]](#cite_note-browse-344) The museum's ASK App allows visitors to talk with staff and educators about works in the collection.[[345]](#cite_note-345)[[346]](#cite_note-Carman_2016_p482-346)

## Attendance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Attendance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Disciples_Having_Left_Their_Hiding_Place_Watch_from_Afar_in_Agony_-_James_Tissot.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Disciples_Having_Left_Their_Hiding_Place_Watch_from_Afar_in_Agony_-_James_Tissot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_The_Disciples_Having_Left_Their_Hiding_Place_Watch_from_Afar_in_Agony_-_James_Tissot.jpg)

[James Tissot](/wiki/James_Tissot "James Tissot"), *The Disciples Having Left Their Hiding Place Watch from Afar in Agony*, c. 1886–1894

Prior to World War II, the museum offered free admission and regularly attracted over a million annual visitors.[[177]](#cite_note-nyt-1993-11-30-177) In 1934, the museum reported 940,000 annual visitors, while its library had 40,000 visitors.[[347]](#cite_note-n128529674-347) Patronage declined along with Brooklyn's economy in the mid-20th century;[[177]](#cite_note-nyt-1993-11-30-177) there were about 470,000 visitors per year by the early 1950s.[[94]](#cite_note-nyt-1951-02-09-94) The museum recorded 1 million visitors in 1971 for the first time in almost four decades.[[348]](#cite_note-348) During the mid-1980s, the museum had 300,000 visitors per year, much less than the [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") or the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") in Manhattan.[[152]](#cite_note-nyt-1985-01-22-152) Annual attendance at the museum, which had stagnated at 250,000 in the mid-1990s, had nearly doubled by 1999 after the museum held several popular exhibits,[[183]](#cite_note-n127641060-183) peaking at 585,000 in 1998.[[349]](#cite_note-NYT1-349) The museum only had 326,000 visitors by 2009,[[349]](#cite_note-NYT1-349) but attendance had increased to 465,000 by 2017.[[204]](#cite_note-nyt-2017-04-30-204)

*The New York Times* attributed the drop in attendance partially to the policies instituted by then-current director Arnold Lehman, who has chosen to focus the museum's energy on "populism", with exhibits on topics such as "[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars") movies and [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop") music"[[349]](#cite_note-NYT1-349) rather than on more classical art topics. Lehman had also brought more controversial exhibits, such as a 1999 [show](/wiki/Sensation_(exhibition) "Sensation (exhibition)") that included [Chris Ofili](/wiki/Chris_Ofili "Chris Ofili")'s infamous dung-decorated *[The Holy Virgin Mary](/wiki/The_Holy_Virgin_Mary "The Holy Virgin Mary")*, to the museum.[[350]](#cite_note-artinfo-350) According to one observer:

> The quality of their exhibitions has lessened", said Robert Storr, the dean of the Yale University School of Art and a Brooklynite. "'*Star Wars*' shows the worst kind of populism. I don't think they really understand where they are. The middle of the art world is now in Brooklyn; it's an increasingly sophisticated audience and always was one.[[349]](#cite_note-NYT1-349)

On the other hand, Lehman says that the demographics of museum attendees are showing a new level of diversity. According to *The New York Times*, "the average age [of museum attendees in a 2008 survey] was 35, a large portion of the visitors (40 percent) came from Brooklyn, and more than 40 percent identified themselves as people of color."[[351]](#cite_note-NYT2-351) Lehman states that the museum's interest is in being welcoming and attractive to all potential museum attendees, rather than simply amassing large numbers of them.[[351]](#cite_note-NYT2-351)

As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Museum&action=edit), the Brooklyn Museum has a [pay what you want](/wiki/Pay_what_you_want "Pay what you want") policy for general-admission tickets.[[352]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Museum_FAQ-352) Half of patrons did not pay any admission in 2017.[[204]](#cite_note-nyt-2017-04-30-204)
